questions.question: Which statement is most clearly a non-functional requirement?
options: Users can upload an image and receive a shareable link.
The system should keep p99 read latency under 150 ms during normal peak traffic.
Moderators can delete abusive content.
Users can search their own uploads by filename.
questions.explanation: Latency is a quality constraint on how the system behaves, not a user-visible feature.

questions.question: Why is it risky to start architecture before separating requirement types?
options: Because interviews require requirements to be written in YAML first.
Because feature scope and quality constraints can blur together, making trade-offs hard to justify.
Because storage choices are always obvious once the prompt is heard.
Because non-functional requirements only matter after implementation.
questions.explanation: Mixing the categories too early makes the real design driver harder to identify.

questions.question: What is the main value of ranking non-functional requirements instead of listing them flatly?
options: It makes the answer sound more formal without changing design choices.
It reveals which quality constraint should drive the first architecture decisions and which ones are secondary.
It removes the need to discuss out-of-scope features.
It guarantees the interviewer will agree with your design.
questions.explanation: Priority creates a usable decision rule for architecture choices.

questions.question: Which mixed prompt would most likely force a trade-off conversation?
options: Support file upload, global low-latency reads, and the cheapest possible storage path.
Support user sign-in and password reset.
Allow read and write APIs for one internal team.
Store one configuration file per tenant.
questions.explanation: Global latency and aggressive cost control often pull the design in different directions.

questions.question: Why should one dominant non-functional driver usually be named early?
options: Because every senior interview only measures one metric.
Because it helps explain why you pick a particular topology, storage model, or failure policy first.
Because functional requirements are usually unimportant.
Because it prevents any follow-up redesign questions.
questions.explanation: A dominant driver gives coherence to early architecture choices.

questions.question: Which failure is most dangerous in this lesson's workflow?
options: Using markdown bullets instead of a table.
Treating every requirement as equally critical and never stating what you would optimize first.
Mentioning both latency and durability in the same answer.
Deferring one secondary feature to a later phase.
questions.explanation: Flat prioritization leads to hand-wavy architecture and weak trade-off discussion.

questions.question: What metric best indicates the clarification process is healthy before architecture starts?
options: The number of database technologies mentioned.
Whether the answer identifies both requirement categories and names a dominant non-functional driver.
The total number of user stories written down.
Whether a queue appears in the diagram.
questions.explanation: The signal is classification plus prioritization, not component selection.

questions.question: If the interviewer adds 'must survive region loss' after you optimized for low latency, what should happen first?
options: Ignore it because architecture already started.
Revisit the ranked non-functional priorities and explain how the new durability requirement changes topology or write path choices.
Immediately switch to a microservices diagram.
Add a cache and continue without discussion.
questions.explanation: A stronger durability requirement can materially change replication and acknowledgement strategy.
